items.q: In an exothermic reaction, what happens energetically with the bonds?
items.correct: Energy released forming new bonds exceeds energy absorbed breaking existing bonds.
items.misconception: Bond breaking releases energy and bond making absorbs energy.
items.random_wrong: The reactants get colder because they lose mass as energy.
items.why: Students confuse the direction: they think breaking is 'releasing' (like cracking something open releases contents), when actually breaking always requires energy input.

items.q: Where is almost all the mass of an atom located?
items.correct: In the nucleus, made up of protons and neutrons.
items.misconception: Electrons contribute significantly to the mass of an atom.
items.random_wrong: Mass is spread evenly throughout the entire atom including the electron shells.
items.why: Students see electrons listed alongside protons and neutrons and assume they contribute comparable mass — in reality an electron has ~1/1836 the mass of a proton.

items.q: What is different between two isotopes of the same element?
items.correct: They have the same number of protons but different numbers of neutrons.
items.misconception: Isotopes have different numbers of electrons, which is why their mass differs.
items.random_wrong: Isotopes are atoms of different elements with the same mass.
items.why: Students conflate isotopes with ions because both alter a property of the atom — but ions change electrons (charge), isotopes change neutrons (mass).

items.q: A sports cold pack contains ammonium nitrate and water. When squeezed, it gets cold. The reaction is.
items.why: It's Endothermic — takes in heat. “Exothermic — releases heat” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Why is the experimental value for a fuel's energy per gram usually lower than the true value?
items.correct: Heat is lost to the surroundings
items.random_wrong: The fuel weighs more than expected
items.why: Correct answer: Heat is lost to the surroundings. “The thermometer reads too high” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Burning 2 g of a fuel releases 84 kJ of heat. What is the energy per gram, in kJ/g?
items.why: The real figure is 42. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
